abstract:
The World Federation of Trade Unions (WFTU), after years of relative obscurity, is re-emerging as a significant presence in the international labor movement. This "second coming" is most visible in South Africa, where several major unions within the ITUC-affiliated COSATU have joined the WFTU and are pushing the federation to do the same. While some observers frame this resurgence as a potential path toward global labor unity, a closer examination reveals a movement defined by "Hydroponic Stalinism"—an ideology sustained artificially, without roots in organic, democratic struggle.

Drawing on the history of the WFTU and its predecessor, the Profintern, this paper argues that the organization remains a vehicle for top-down, state-controlled unionism. Despite current General Secretary George Mavrikos’s efforts to rebrand the WFTU as a modern, "class-oriented" revolutionary alternative, its core remains anchored in the remnants of the Soviet model and the interests of authoritarian regimes in China, Vietnam, and Cuba. Meanwhile, the ITUC’s conciliatory and depoliticized stance has created a vacuum on the left that the WFTU seeks to exploit. By analyzing recent clashes at COSATU congresses and Mavrikos’s aggressive rhetoric, this article suggests that the "fundamental difference" between these two internationals is not merely tactical, but a deep-seated historical divide between independent worker self-organization and the legacy of bureaucratic state control. Ultimately, true global labor solidarity is more likely to emerge from the organic, grassroots struggles of workers themselves than from the artificial structures of these competing bureaucracies.
